publicReleaseLedger.surface: The assay is plain in the cupel: these shillings are no clipped sterling but a struck coin of poor dross — silver eked out with too much copper and a grey lead-sweat the touchstone catches at once. Clipping shaves a true coin; it strikes no new one. These were struck.

The founder's man knows that dross by its lead-sweat: it answers to the leavings of one crucible on all this coast — the weir-forge crucible above the mill-leat, cold these ten years since the old founder died and his yard was shut.

Lay a dozen of the light shillings under the glass and they share one fault to the hair: a broken letter in the legend, the serif of the R struck with a small square notch where the graver slipped and was never mended. One die struck them all.

The guild's die-sinker reads that notch like a hand he knows: it is no slip of the burnisher but the bite of a worn burin with a sprung heel — a tool that signs every line it cuts with that square notch. It is not the broad graver from Verrell's bench, which cuts clean.

analyzer_public_input.topic: Whose hand struck the false shillings passed at the Marrick fair?

world.discipline: Moneyer's assay (a trial of false coin)
world.question: Whose hand struck the false shillings passed at the Marrick fair?

rules.gloss: A struck coin's blank betrays its melt: if a coin is of a debased alloy, and that very alloy answers to the leavings of one crucible and one only, then the coin's blank was cast from that crucible.
A blank is the work of the hand that cast it: if a coin's blank came from a crucible, and one hand alone cast at that crucible, then that hand cast the blank for the coin.
A die leaves its flaw on every coin it strikes: if a coin bears a die-flaw, and that flaw is the signature of one graving-tool and one only, then the coin's die was cut with that tool.
A die is the work of the hand that held the tool: if a coin's die was cut with a tool, and one hand alone held that tool, then that hand cut the die for the coin.

To strike false coin needs both the cast blank and the cut die in one hand: who had the blank cast for a coin and the die cut for it, that one struck it. A blank without a die strikes nothing, and a die without a blank strikes nothing; both must meet in one hand.

world.setting: The autumn fair at Marrick passed in light shillings — coin that rings wrong and bends to the tooth, found in every second till when the booths came down. The mint warden has convened an assay in the guild-hall, and the town has settled its verdict before the crucible is lit: coin is the coiner's work, and Marrick has a coiner — Verrell, twice in the pillory for clipping, with a graver on his bench and a crucible at the mint-yard. You are the assay-master's apprentice, sent to keep the trial-book, quick with the touchstone and quicker to convict the man the town already hates. The assay trusts its rules of evidence, and nothing else.
world.title: The Light Shillings

cases.learner_text: Because the operative seal on the Leadenhall gate-pass is the raven's-notch dusk-seal pressed beneath the wax, the hand that held and pressed that particular seal is the hand that signed the pass.

publicReleaseLedger.surface: Hold the pass to the slant light: under the red wax is a second, older impression, the little dusk-seal used after curfew - a raven's notch under the wing, pressed before the public ribbon was laid across it.

analyzer_public_input.topic: Whose hand signed the Leadenhall gate-pass that opened the river gate at dusk?

world.discipline: Borough seal inquiry (gate records and warrants)
world.question: Whose hand signed the Leadenhall gate-pass that opened the river gate at dusk?

rules.gloss: The pass-law asks for a hand on a seal, not merely an officer in charge: if a pass carries a named seal-mark, then the material seal is the point at issue.
A material seal points to its holder: if the seal at issue on a pass is a seal held by one hand, then that hand pressed the pass.
For a sealed gate-pass, the signature belongs to the hand that pressed the operative seal. The scribe may write the letters; the seal signs the warrant.
A steward with custody of the gate and the gate-key answers for a pass that opens it. Answerability names who must account; it does not name the hand that signed.

world.setting: The Leadenhall river gate opened at dusk for a cart that should have waited until morning, and the town's missing coffer went out under that cart's tilt. The council has its answer ready: Corvin, the gate-steward, kept the gate and answered for every pass that crossed it. You are the junior clerk of the seal table, set to read the warrant book before the aldermen turn a ready suspicion into a sentence. The table trusts its rules of evidence, and nothing else.
world.title: The Ravensmark Pass
